lg.l: The flaming face of the God [under] command opened.
[All] the guardian deities closed the entrance-gates.
Ceḻiyaṉ of deadly battles, the great king, having given up
[His] very life because of the violation of the [now] bent sceptre,
As he showed the [once-]straight sceptre to the lady of the wide lands,
Knowing not that the king had fallen upon the bed along with the queen
Of incomparable chastity, all the people of the palace —
The teachers, the great astrologers, brahmins of the domain of dharma
Along with those who make note of mantras [according to]
The rules of protection, and women [with] short bracelets,
Ceasing [all] speech, like a gathering of [mere] paintings,
Those with goads [=animal tamers], mahouts, those who mount
Fast chariots [and] warriors [with] excellent swords, crowding together
As those who were distressed, having seen the fiery face [at]
The victorious entrance of the palace of the king, taking leave,
[Appeared] the superior deity of the city, the principal Bhūta,
As the foremost one of the Vedas, along with [his] sacrificial instruments,
Not deviating from the manner of a livelihood of the three fires,
He had a body like the moon [with] cool rays that shine, glittering,
Decorated with pearls of bright rays, him with a head of a good colour,
That was knotted pleasantly with these, namely, white-coloured lotuses
Aṟukai grass [and] Nanti flowers; he had dress that does not dry up,
[Of] fine, beautiful Kaliṅgam cloth that expanded like foam;
He had a chest that possessed the Aṭṭikai necklace that did not bloom,
[And] sandalwood [applied with] a pen [as] bright spots,
Having been rubbed [with] a stone; he consumed the sweet smoke
That was obtained with those that were combined with appreciation
[Namely] honey, milk and lumps of jaggery [into the fire];
Standing along with [everyone else] [in] the shores of the water,
The temple of Gods and the chanting-halls, he would move
With desire/respect, in the afternoon. He was one with a leg
That leaned, [his] feet [standing in one place] in the good noon.
He had hands that gripped an expanding parasol, a staff,
A pitcher, a small stick and the inseparable taruppai grass.
He was one with a thread/treatise that was announced [= visible]
On his tongue and chest [respectively]. And [then appeared]
The deity of rare strength, the protective deity of kings:
He had a body that resembled [the one with] victorious, hot rays.
He wore adornments of undiminishing sapphires. Along with
[Those] ornaments, he was one who wore jewels, starting with
A crown. Along with the crown, he was one with flowers that shook,
[With] a stringing of Campaka, Karuviḷai [and] red Kūṭāḷam, along with
Cāti [that grows in] the water, [with] flowers that emit a cool fragrance,
[And] other such of these; a chaplet, and a garland that was strung together,
Along with a garland of flower-balls that were stuck [together].
He had a chest that possessed the colour of vermillion, that had spread
All five of the fingers [in his] hand having spread [too]. He possessed
Silks [soft like] red flowers, with overflowing lustre. Holding up
A golden vessel [with] food [from] paddy befitting [to resemble] buds,
He is one who stays, taking heat (?), having brought tasty tamarind
That is suitably good; he has a body that shines, resplendent,
[Like] the red glow of coral; he is one that rules the world
[With] the deep seas; along with his Muracu drum, being one
Who is with hands that hold these that are a white parasol,
A chowrie, a red banner, an elephant goad that is worthy of fame,
A sharp spear [and] a sharp whip, having driven away kings
Of an excellence that is difficult to count, taking control of
The earth, raising the straight sceptre [of justice], chasing away
Evil deeds, taking victory, growing lasting fame, [and]
Resembling the Tall One (Neṭiyōṉ) of an excellence worthy of narration,
Who protects the four lands. [Then appeared] the great protective deity
Shining with light, — the Lord who wore on [his] brightly shining forehead
A young moon, he who was called the master of a faultless life,
He had a body [like] superior gold, [with] a fine colour.
He was one who wore, having established [a crown], so that
The royal crowns of kings [with] brave spears, of an excellence
That was permanent, stayed behind; having protected
The eternal world, as a member/in the tradition of the merchant class,
He had hands that held up a plough and a weighing scale;
He had clothes that possessed the colour of gold, worthy of fame;
He was one with a head onto which was knotted, combining
Veṭci, the core of screwprine, fragrant waterlily, Cēṭal,
Blue waterlily, silk-cotton [and] Marutam; he has a chest
Of the colour of lightning, that was adorned with sandal
Of a good colour that was spread like gold of long-lasting lustre;
He is the one who sits, who takes, saying ‘let’s take!’
Mixing with appreciation, having [also] accepted horsegram,
Green gram, pigeon peas, black gram and many [more];
It is him who eats, [his] heart rejoicing, in the forenoon,
Using [his] hand, [at] the fields possessing paddy for threshing,
[Or] paddy fields possessing birds, at the shop counters of merchants,
[Or under] the Kāñci tree [with] a long shade; having gained
The pretty weapon [that was] the plough, having also lifted
The lute that sounded brightly before the weighing scale
[The harvest] ripening, food increasing, [he] having given
A feast of food, having [also] brought rare bounties -
Those of the mountains and those of the seas - bestowing [them]
To those who want as items for sale. [And then], he who is called
The great hero/head of protective deities of sacrificial offerings
Having appeared in Kūṭal that full of bustling:
He had a body that resembled the Karuviḷai flower;
He had jewels that were adorned with streaks of silver;
He had clothes in which clear, bright Kāḻakam cloth was densely packed;
He had a chest a wide chest, a paste of hard eaglewood having dried on it;
He had a hair tuft in which flowers, available from high mountains
From creepers, from water and from land were mixed; lifting up
A plough made by artisans, joining in [with] excellence into [that which]
Would come [with] excellence, [namely], he with a body that resembles
Polished, brilliant sapphires - the one with clothes to which brightly coloured
Kāḻakam cloth was joined - having come together with those [instruments]
That were suitable for dancing, having [then] gone to many sacred ghats
That were suitable for being sung about; [and then]
The Pūtams of four types, moving away in sections before the victorious lady
Who singled out [her] breast, saying, Because we know
As per the order [of things] that is, that there is a side
[That foretells] the consuming of this city [by] the order of the fire,
On the [same] day in which the order of the king failed, isn’t it nature
That we go [away because of that] order [of the fire]?’
The granary streets, the streets [with] chariots [with] flags, and
The four distinct streets in which the sections [of the different castes]
Was clear, having become stirred up like the day in which the strong one
[With] a bright bow which raised [a sigil of] a strong monkey
Consumed the forest [with] flames, the creeper of fire was not let [into]
